title: Saganaki
subtitle: Pan-fried Greek cheese with lemon and oregano.
excerpt: A small slab of Greek hard cheese floured and pan-fried in olive oil until the outside browns and the inside softens. Lemon wedge and dried oregano on the plate; ouzo or cold beer alongside. Ten minutes total. The classic Greek mezze opener.
sourceNotes:
Greek mezze tradition, named after the small two-handled frying pan (sagani) the dish is cooked in. Documented in Greek and Greek-Cypriot cookbooks throughout the twentieth century.

The cheese varies by region. Mainland Greece favours kefalograviera or kefalotyri; the islands often use graviera; halloumi is the Cypriot stand-in. All three brown well; soft cheeses such as feta do not work — they collapse in the pan.

Flaming saganaki with brandy and crying "opa" is a Chicago-Greek invention from the 1960s, not Greek tradition. Skip unless your dinner needs theatre.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Saganaki is cooked to order. The crust softens within five minutes of leaving the pan; build it as the table is sitting down.

recipeTools.notes: A heavy 15 to 18 cm pan, ideally cast-iron or heavy non-stick. The pan should be smaller than the cheese slab plus a fingerwidth around.
A thin metal fish slice slides under the crust without tearing it.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hard, salty Greek sheep's-milk cheese used for grating and frying. Lower-moisture than feta, with a crust-forming character when fried. Substitutes include kefalotyri, graviera, or Cypriot halloumi if Greek options are not to hand.

content.text: Saganaki is a piece of Greek hard cheese the size of a small phone, floured and fried in olive oil until the outside is a crackling dark gold and the inside has softened to a creamy chew. Lemon wedge, dried oregano, eat off the pan. Ten minutes from cold cheese to first bite. The standard opening course in any Greek taverna.
The cheese has to be a hard, salty, low-moisture one that forms a crust before melting through. The mainland choice is
kefalograviera
. Greek shops stock it; outside Greece, kefalotyri, graviera, or Cypriot halloumi all work. Feta does not; it crumbles before it browns.

The pan should be smaller than the cheese plus a fingerwidth around — the cheese touches the edges and the oil sits in a shallow puddle. A heavy small frying pan, ideally cast-iron, holds the heat where a thin pan loses it the moment the cheese hits.
What you need

items.prepNote: kefalograviera, graviera, or halloumi — sliced 1.5 cm thick in one or two slabs

content.text: Note on the slug: the master ingredient list flags this row as feta because no separate slug exists for the hard Greek frying cheeses. The recipe still wants kefalograviera, kefalotyri, graviera, or halloumi; feta is in the list for substitution lookup, not for use here.
Method
Dunk the cheese, then dredge

Run the cheese slab under cold water for a moment so the surface is damp. Tip {{plain-flour}} of plain flour onto a plate. Lay the wet cheese in the flour, turn to coat all sides, then shake off the excess. A thin even coat is what you want; clumps of flour burn.
Heat the pan and oil

Set a heavy small frying pan over a medium-high heat. Pour in {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and let it heat for a minute until the surface shimmers. The oil is ready when a pinch of flour dropped in fizzes and browns within five seconds; cooler than that and the crust will not catch.
Fry until the crust catches

Lay the cheese carefully into the oil, away from you. Listen for the immediate fizz; if there is no fizz, the oil is not hot enough and the cheese will leak. Fry for ninety seconds to two minutes on one side without touching it. The flour goes from pale to deep gold and you can see the edges turn slightly translucent as the inside softens.
Slide the fish slice under one corner; the crust should release. Turn the cheese in one move and fry the second side for ninety seconds. Lift onto a warm plate or transfer the whole pan to the table.
Lemon and oregano

Squeeze {{lemon}} of lemon over the cheese the moment it is on the plate. The acid cuts the salt and the fat in a single move. Sprinkle {{oregano-dried}} of dried oregano on top. A few grinds of black pepper. Eat straight away, with bread and a cold drink.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: The two failure modes are cheese that leaks (oil not hot enough) and crust that burns black (oil too hot or flour too thick).

items.symptom: Cheese melts into the oil instead of browning.
items.cause: Oil was below frying temperature when the cheese went in.
items.fix: Test with a pinch of flour first; it should fizz and brown within five seconds. If the oil is cool, lift the cheese out, dry it, re-flour, and start again with hotter oil.

items.symptom: Crust burned almost black in under a minute.
items.fix: Drop the heat and let the pan cool for a minute before going again. Olive oil starts to smoke and bitter around 190°C; a steady medium-high is what you want, not full blast.

items.symptom: Flour clumped on the surface in dark patches.
items.cause: Too much flour or cheese surface too wet.
items.fix: Pat the cheese after the cold-water dip if it is dripping; you want damp, not wet. Shake the cheese hard after flouring to drop loose flour.
Saganaki wants a hard, salty cheese: kefalograviera, kefalotyri, graviera, or halloumi. Soft feta or mozzarella will collapse and disappoint.

content.text: Variations

Some tavernas top the fried cheese with a spoon of honey and a scatter of sesame seeds for a sweet-salt opener. Others add a smear of fig jam or sliced fresh fig. Both work, both are modern, both move the dish toward dessert territory.
Cypriot halloumi gets the same treatment without the flour dredge; halloumi has a higher melting point and holds shape on its own. Use the same lemon and oregano finish.
Where this dish lives

Saganaki is named after the small two-handled frying pan it is cooked in, the sagani. The pan and the dish share the name, and the dish moves to the table still in the pan in most Greek tavernas. It is the canonical first plate of a mezze spread, sitting alongside tzatziki, olives, and bread before the grills arrive.

The flaming version with brandy and shouts of opa is a Chicago-Greek restaurant invention from the 1960s. It became a tourist sight in some Greek restaurants in the years that followed but has no traditional root. The dish at home is quieter and the result is the same.
